title: Tick Sound (Web Audio API — Three Layered Oscillators)
deliverable: lib/audio.js exports playTickSound() that synthesises a three-layer tick on device via Web Audio API with no audio files. The function fires on every task completion (wired into Block 4's completion handler). Zero latency, works fully offline, handles mobile Safari autoplay policy.

in: Single shared AudioContext created lazily on first user gesture
Mobile Safari AudioContext unlock via user-gesture listener
Layer 1: sharp click oscillator, frequency sweep ~1800→2400 Hz, ~80ms duration
Layer 2: warm rising sine tone, frequency sweep ~880→1320 Hz, ~300ms duration
Layer 3: high sparkle oscillator, frequency sweep ~3200→4200 Hz, ~350ms duration
Gain envelopes (attack + decay ramps) on every layer to prevent clicks and pops
Wire playTickSound() call into the Block 4 completion handler (Queue.jsx or useQueue.js)
Graceful no-op when Web Audio API is unavailable (SSR safety, very old browsers)
Resume suspended AudioContext before scheduling oscillators
out: Volume settings UI or user-facing audio controls
Any oscillator type beyond the three specified layers

files.purpose: Exports playTickSound(). Holds module-level AudioContext singleton. Implements three-oscillator synthesis with gain envelopes.

Import playTickSound and call it inside the completion handler (after user tap, before or at the start of the 500ms animation delay). Also add a one-time user-gesture listener to unlock AudioContext on mount.

implementation_steps.description: Create src/lib/audio.js with AudioContext singleton management
implementation_steps.details: Declare a module-level variable `let _ctx = null`. Export a private helper `getAudioContext()` that: (a) checks `typeof AudioContext !== 'undefined' || typeof webkitAudioContext !== 'undefined'`; (b) if _ctx is null, creates `new (window.AudioContext || window.webkitAudioContext)()`; (c) if `_ctx.state === 'suspended'`, calls `_ctx.resume()` (returns a Promise — do not await, fire and forget); (d) returns _ctx. If Web Audio API is absent, return null. The context is never re-created — one instance for the app lifetime.

implementation_steps.description: Implement mobile Safari unlock: export unlockAudio() to be called on first user gesture
implementation_steps.details: Export `function unlockAudio()`. Inside: call `getAudioContext()`. If the returned ctx is non-null and `ctx.state === 'suspended'`, call `ctx.resume()`. Additionally create a zero-duration buffer source, connect to ctx.destination, start it, and immediately stop it — this is the standard iOS Safari AudioContext unlock pattern required before any audio can play. This function must be called from a direct user-gesture event handler (e.g. onClick, onTouchEnd).

implementation_steps.description: Implement Layer 1 — sharp click oscillator (~1800→2400 Hz, ~80ms)
implementation_steps.details: Inside playTickSound(), obtain ctx via getAudioContext(); if null, return early. Create `osc1 = ctx.createOscillator()` with `osc1.type = 'sawtooth'` (sawtooth gives the transient sharpness of a click). Create `gain1 = ctx.createGain()`. Connect osc1 → gain1 → ctx.destination. Gain envelope: at `now`, set gain to 0 via `gain1.gain.setValueAtTime(0, now)`; ramp to 0.35 at `now + 0.003` (3ms attack to avoid DC click); ramp to 0 at `now + 0.080` (exponential or linear decay over 80ms). Frequency sweep: `osc1.frequency.setValueAtTime(1800, now)`; `osc1.frequency.linearRampToValueAtTime(2400, now + 0.080)`. Start at `now`; stop at `now + 0.085`.

implementation_steps.description: Implement Layer 2 — warm rising sine tone (~880→1320 Hz, ~300ms)
implementation_steps.details: Create `osc2 = ctx.createOscillator()` with `osc2.type = 'sine'`. Create `gain2 = ctx.createGain()`. Connect osc2 → gain2 → ctx.destination. Gain envelope: setValueAtTime(0, now); linearRampToValueAtTime(0.22, now + 0.010) (10ms attack); linearRampToValueAtTime(0.18, now + 0.150) (gentle sustain slope); linearRampToValueAtTime(0, now + 0.300) (tail to silence). Frequency sweep: `osc2.frequency.setValueAtTime(880, now)`; `osc2.frequency.linearRampToValueAtTime(1320, now + 0.300)`. Start at `now`; stop at `now + 0.310`.

implementation_steps.description: Implement Layer 3 — high sparkle oscillator (~3200→4200 Hz, ~350ms)
implementation_steps.details: Create `osc3 = ctx.createOscillator()` with `osc3.type = 'triangle'` (triangle at high frequencies gives an airy sparkle without harshness). Create `gain3 = ctx.createGain()`. Connect osc3 → gain3 → ctx.destination. Gain envelope: setValueAtTime(0, now); linearRampToValueAtTime(0.12, now + 0.008) (8ms attack, lower amplitude so sparkle is subtle not piercing); linearRampToValueAtTime(0.10, now + 0.200); linearRampToValueAtTime(0, now + 0.350). Frequency sweep: `osc3.frequency.setValueAtTime(3200, now)`; `osc3.frequency.linearRampToValueAtTime(4200, now + 0.350)`. Start at `now`; stop at `now + 0.360`. The staggered stop times (0.085 / 0.310 / 0.360) mean all oscillators self-clean; no manual disconnect needed (GC handles after stop).

implementation_steps.description: Export playTickSound() as the public API
implementation_steps.details: The exported function contains steps 5.3–5.5 sequentially. It captures `const now = ctx.currentTime` once at the top, then builds all three layers using that same `now` value so they fire simultaneously. The function is synchronous (no async/await). It returns void. Add a try/catch around the entire body: on any error, silently swallow it (audio failure must never crash the app or surface to the user).

implementation_steps.description: Wire unlockAudio() into Queue.jsx on mount
implementation_steps.details: In Queue.jsx (or the top-level App.jsx if Queue mounts immediately), import `unlockAudio` from `lib/audio`. In a useEffect with empty deps `[]`, add a one-time event listener for `'touchstart'` and `'mousedown'` on `window` that calls `unlockAudio()` and then removes itself (`{ once: true }` option or manual removeEventListener). This ensures the AudioContext is unlocked on the very first interaction, before the user taps a task checkbox.

implementation_steps.description: Wire playTickSound() into Block 4 completion handler
implementation_steps.details: Locate the completion handler in Queue.jsx (or useQueue.js, whichever Block 4 placed it). Import `playTickSound` from `lib/audio`. Call `playTickSound()` synchronously at the very start of the handler, before the 500ms animation delay. Rationale: audio must fire with zero perceptible latency at the moment of tap. The animation delay is for visual reward; audio is immediate. The call order is: (1) playTickSound(), (2) begin card exit animation, (3) after ~500ms delay, update state.

implementation_steps.description: Verify no audio files are bundled and offline operation
implementation_steps.details: Confirm via `npm run build` output that no .mp3/.ogg/.wav files appear in the dist directory. Confirm lib/audio.js has zero `import` statements referencing audio assets. Web Audio synthesis is 100% runtime — the service worker from Block 15 does not need to cache any audio resource for the tick to work offline.

data_structures.name: AudioContext singleton
data_structures.shape: let _ctx: AudioContext | null = null  (module-level in lib/audio.js)
data_structures.notes: Never re-created. Shared across all calls to playTickSound(). Created lazily — only on first getAudioContext() call, which itself is called either from unlockAudio() on first gesture or from playTickSound() on first completion, whichever comes first.

data_structures.name: OscillatorNode (per layer, per call)
data_structures.notes: Three instances created fresh on every playTickSound() call. They are not stored or reused. After stop() the browser GC reclaims them. Creating new oscillators per call is the correct Web Audio pattern — stopped oscillators cannot be restarted.

data_structures.name: GainNode (per layer, per call)
data_structures.notes: One per oscillator. Envelope is scheduled via AudioParam automation methods (setValueAtTime, linearRampToValueAtTime). Determines both the amplitude shape and prevents clicks at start/end.

interfaces.name: playTickSound
interfaces.purpose: Public API. Called from the completion handler on every task tick. Synthesises and plays the three-layer tick sound using the shared AudioContext. Silently no-ops if Web Audio API unavailable or if any error occurs.

interfaces.name: unlockAudio
interfaces.purpose: Must be called from a direct user-gesture handler (touchstart/mousedown). Resumes the suspended AudioContext and runs the iOS Safari silent-buffer unlock trick. After this call, playTickSound() will produce audio on all mobile browsers.

interfaces.name: getAudioContext (private)
interfaces.purpose: Internal helper. Lazily creates the singleton AudioContext, handles the webkitAudioContext prefix for Safari, resumes a suspended context, and returns null if Web Audio API is absent.

acceptance_criteria: Tapping the task checkbox triggers an audible three-layer tick sound on desktop Chrome/Firefox/Safari within ≤16ms of the tap event
The sound is clearly distinguishable as three simultaneous layers: a short sharp click, a rising warm tone, and a fading high sparkle — not a single beep
No audio pops, clicks, or distortion at the start or end of any layer
On iOS Safari 16+: after the first tap anywhere on the page, subsequent task completions produce the tick sound
With network offline and the app loaded from cache (PWA), task completion still produces the tick sound (no network request for audio)
No .mp3, .ogg, .wav, or any audio asset file is present in the build output (dist/)
If Web Audio API is unavailable, the completion handler still completes normally — no error is thrown, no UI breakage
A second completion immediately after the first (rapid tapping) also plays the tick without error — new oscillator nodes created fresh each call
The AudioContext is not created on page load (before any user gesture) — creation is deferred to first gesture or first completion
verification: Open DevTools → Network tab → set to Offline → complete a task → confirm tick plays
Open DevTools → Sources → search dist/ for any file with extension .mp3/.ogg/.wav → expect zero results
Open DevTools → Console → complete a task → expect zero errors or warnings
On an iOS device (real hardware, not simulator): load the PWA → tap any area → complete a task → confirm audible tick
Use DevTools → Performance → record a completion event → verify playTickSound() execution time is <5ms
Add console.log(ctx.state) inside getAudioContext() → on page load before any gesture, confirm _ctx is null (not yet created) → after first gesture confirm ctx.state === 'running'
Complete 5 tasks in rapid succession → confirm each produces a tick without AudioContext errors in console
In a browser without Web Audio API (mock via `delete window.AudioContext; delete window.webkitAudioContext`) → complete a task → confirm no throw, app behaves normally
Run `npm run build` → inspect dist/ via `find dist/ -name '*.mp3' -o -name '*.ogg' -o -name '*.wav'` → expect empty output

questionable_decisions.decision: Create fresh OscillatorNode instances on every playTickSound() call rather than reusing stopped nodes
questionable_decisions.assumption: Web Audio spec does not allow restarting a stopped OscillatorNode, so new nodes must be created per call. GC overhead for short-lived nodes at tap frequency (at most a few per second) is negligible.
alternatives: Pre-create a pool of oscillators and rotate them
Use AudioBufferSourceNode with a rendered buffer (render once, reuse)
questionable_decisions.recommendation: Keep fresh-creation approach. It is the idiomatic Web Audio pattern, simpler, and tap frequency is far too low to cause GC pressure.

questionable_decisions.decision: Call playTickSound() synchronously at the start of the completion handler, before the 500ms animation delay
questionable_decisions.assumption: Audio feedback must feel instantaneous — firing it after the animation delay would make it feel disconnected from the tap. The animation is the visual reward; audio is the tactile/auditory reward and needs no delay.
alternatives: Play sound after the 500ms delay (aligned with state update)
Play sound at the midpoint of the card exit animation (~250ms)
questionable_decisions.recommendation: Keep immediate firing. Zero-latency audio is a stated PRD requirement (§3.2, §7.3). Any delay defeats the dopamine-loop design intent.

questionable_decisions.decision: Use sawtooth for Layer 1, sine for Layer 2, triangle for Layer 3 (oscillator types not explicitly stated in PRD)
questionable_decisions.assumption: PRD specifies frequency ranges and durations but not waveform types. Sawtooth best approximates a sharp transient click; sine is the warmest/purest tone; triangle at high frequency gives airy sparkle without harshness.
alternatives: All three layers as sine waves (softer overall character, less click punch)
Layer 1 as square wave (harsher click, potentially unpleasant)
questionable_decisions.recommendation: Keep sawtooth/sine/triangle. Matches the described character (sharp/warm/sparkle). If the sound feels wrong on real devices, Layer 1 can be tuned to 'square' or 'sine' at the implementation phase without changing the spec structure.

questionable_decisions.decision: unlockAudio() is attached to window touchstart/mousedown via useEffect in Queue.jsx rather than in a global App.jsx provider
questionable_decisions.assumption: Queue.jsx is the only component where completions occur (Block 4 lives there). Mounting the unlock listener there is sufficient. If the architecture later moves completion logic elsewhere, the listener location may need to move.
alternatives: Create an AudioProvider context and attach the unlock listener in App.jsx at the root (more robust, survives future refactors)
Attach directly in the checkbox onClick handler on first call (simpler, but onClick may not fire fast enough on all iOS versions)
questionable_decisions.recommendation: Move the unlock listener to App.jsx for robustness. Since App.jsx is always mounted first and user gestures can happen anywhere (not just on the queue), App.jsx is the correct attachment point. Spec should reflect App.jsx as the location.

questionable_decisions.decision: Gain amplitudes set to 0.35 / 0.22 / 0.12 across the three layers (Layer 1 loudest, Layer 3 quietest)
questionable_decisions.assumption: High-frequency content (Layer 3 at 3200–4200 Hz) is perceptually louder per unit gain than low-to-mid frequencies, so it is given a lower gain value to prevent it from dominating or causing harshness. These values are initial estimates and will need tuning by ear.
alternatives: Equal gains across all layers (risks Layer 3 being piercing)
Expose gain values as constants at the top of audio.js for easy tuning without structural changes
questionable_decisions.recommendation: Expose all gain peak values and frequency sweep endpoints as named constants at the top of audio.js (e.g. L1_GAIN_PEAK, L2_FREQ_START, etc.). This makes ear-tuning trivial without touching the implementation logic.

open_questions: Should the tick sound respect the device's system volume, or should it always play at the synthesised gain levels? (Currently: system volume governs — no app-level volume control is in scope.)

If the user is wearing headphones at high volume, are the gain values safe/comfortable? The sawtooth Layer 1 at 2400 Hz could be startling. Consider a brief user-test before finalising gain constants.

Block 4 spec location of the completion handler: is it in Queue.jsx directly, or delegated to useQueue.js? This affects the exact import/call site for playTickSound() in step 5.8. Confirm with Block 4 spec before implementation.
Should unlockAudio() be called from App.jsx (recommended in Q4) or Queue.jsx as initially drafted? Resolve before writing implementation to avoid a follow-up file modification.

PWA service worker (Block 15) caches the app shell. lib/audio.js will be bundled into the JS chunk. Confirm the cache-first strategy in Block 15 covers all JS chunks so the audio module is available offline after the first load.
